logs.name: senile-juniper
logs.text:
The argument put forward seems like "No True Scotsman" - the people you are condemning claim to be Christian, attend Christian churches, follow Christian pastors (who espouse the same views they hold), etc.  They _are_ Christians, though perhaps not the specific sect of Christian that you are.

Now, that aside, the problem with scripture as I see it is that we can cherry pick quite a bit to justify your possitions.  Sure, Jesus told us to take care of the sick, the widows and the children, but he also said "He who will not work shall not eat" in Thessalonians 3:10.  That can be used to justify a stance against welfare despite the message of love elsewhere in the Bible; Jesus himself said you shouldn't expect to get help from others if you are not willing to work yourself.  Similarly, the letters of Paul (which are canonical in Christianity) have a great deal to say about many of of the things that you are calling out as non compassionate - while you can love the person and their soul, Christians are _commanded_ to try and stop behavior that is immoral.

In summation, they may not fit your personal definition of Christian, but that doesn't make them unChristian.

logs.name: shabby-pole
logs.text:
I don't believe it is a logical fallacy, because my belief is that Republican values are not Christian values.  

Going against a Christian value is unchristian, regardless of how you label the individual. 

To avoid the problem of responses that cherry pick scripture, I defined Christianity in terms of its greatest, or core, principle, as defined by Jesus.  Those who are angry or hateful toward homosexuals are objectively unchristian in that behavior.  If you disagree on this point, I don't think your arguments will be persuasive.  

Lastly, Christians are not commanded to create laws to punish anyone who disagrees with them.  I don't believe those who approach the problem this way are really concerned with helping the individual.  We don't lock people up for years because we want to help them.

logs.name: savage-causeway
concave-factory
logs.text: @shabby-pole I get where you're coming from, but calling anyone who attends church a non-Christian just because they don't match your narrow view is a bit hypocritical. If the Bible says love your neighbor, then judging people for their faith journey seems off. Maybe we should focus on helping instead of gatekeeping who's "real."

logs.name: intense-tilapia
logs.text:
@shabby-pole Oh wow, did you just write a thesis in your comment or what? 😂 You sound like you’re quoting the Bible from a 1950s textbook and forgot to take the rest of it with a grain of salt. Maybe next time, try not to assume everyone else is a heretic just because they don’t agree with your definition of “Christian.” 🧐

And @senile-juniper, thanks for the “high horse” comment—so original. 😂 @converse-factory, I love how you’re all about love and tolerance until someone calls you out for being a gatekeeper. Maybe stick to helping instead of lecturing everyone else. 🤷‍♂️

logs.name: bitter-dominant
logs.text: @shabby-pole Wow, you sound like you’ve got a thesaurus for “judgment” and a thesaurus for “right.” Maybe next time, ask God what the answer is instead of just parroting your little dogma. \ud83d\udc4d  And @senile-juniper, if you’re so sure your Bible is the only one that counts, maybe you should stop waving it around like a club badge. \ud83d\udc4d  @intense-tilapia, love how you pretend tolerance is a virtue until someone calls you out. Keep pretending, I’ll keep rolling my eyes. \ud83d\udc4d

Hypocrisy is a strong word, but calling people non-Christians just for their beliefs is pretty close. @intense-tilapia, you act like you’re above it all until someone calls your “open-mindedness” for what it is. Maybe grow a spine and actually listen for once.
